topic: A person finds out their biography was written without their knowledge
raw_output:
[story 1]

The crisp, ivory paper felt cold beneath my fingertips as I unfolded the document. Every line, every carefully curated anecdote about my supposed triumphs and quiet virtues, was a stranger's voice. My breath hitched, a shallow, useless thing in my chest. I traced a passage describing my 'unwavering dedication' to a field I’d only dabbled in, a field I’d abandoned months ago out of sheer exhaustion. A tremor started in my hands, making the edges of the paper blur. Looking up, I saw the empty room, the silence amplifying the frantic drumming in my ears. How could someone have painted such a vibrant, impressive portrait of me when I felt like a faded photograph left too long in the sun? I crumpled the paper slowly, the sound a pathetic rustle in the quiet apartment.

[story 2]

It started as a misplaced file, an accidental click on an old archive, but what followed was a slow, sickening descent. The text detailed my entire life, from the mundane beginnings to supposed milestones, all meticulously crafted. Reading it felt like watching someone else inhabit my skin, wearing a costume I never chose. A dull ache settled deep in my stomach, heavy and inescapable. I leaned back in my chair, the cheap fabric groaning under my weight. My jaw tightened until my teeth ached. Every carefully constructed facade I’d spent years maintaining began to crack, revealing a raw, exposed core of inadequacy. I stared at my reflection in the dark monitor screen, searching for the person the document described, finding only a hollow echo staring back.

[story 3]

The revelation hit me like a physical blow—a sudden, sharp drop in temperature. I sat on the floor, hugging my knees to my chest, the texture of the worn carpet rough against my skin. The narrative was a masterpiece of omission and exaggeration, a tapestry woven from threads I didn't own. A strange numbness spread through my limbs, making movement feel laborious, as if my bones were filled with sand. I kept re-reading the passage where they described my supposed 'innate brilliance.' My throat constricted; I tried to speak, but only a dry, rasping sound escaped. I slowly pushed myself up, my gaze fixed on the ceiling, feeling utterly disconnected, as if I were an object observed rather than a being experiencing life. The air felt too thin to draw in.
